title: Banks still falling short, charging dead people, says watchdog
full_text:
A mystery Australian bank asked a grieving family to provide a list of accounts and products held by the deceased before searching its systems, according to the Banking Code Compliance Committee.
A report by the watchdog found three banks continue to fall short of the industry’s agreed standards in how they should handle bank accounts of dead customers.
The BCCC findings set the field for a possible referral to the corporate regulator if the three banks fail to rehabilitate their approach. The committee oversees Australia’s voluntary banking code and was set up in the wake of the royal commission when the banking sector was hammered over charging fees to dead people. Banks have promised to stop.
Recently, the BCCC took aim at the Bank of Queensland and ANZ for the practice. ANZ paid a $35m fine after the corporate regulator took action over the bank’s failure to refund fees charged to thousands of dead customers and inadequately responding to their families.
BCCC chair Sean Hughes told The Australian it was disappointing that three of the 11 code member banks continued to fall short. “Our work is not done and we need to continue to follow up to make sure this part of the banking code is being adhered to by the very people who wrote it,” he said.
Mr Hughes said some banks had indicated they would improve their conduct, but he said this had to be quick.
A key sticking point is banks finding their systems do not provide a consistent view of all the financial products customers hold.
The BCCC found this was particularly bad when legacy platforms or subsidiary brands were involved. To deal with this, staff must conduct multiple searches.
In one case, the BCCC found one of its 11 member banks did not even do a search upfront.
Instead, representatives of a deceased estate had to “identify whether the deceased customer held accounts or other products with other parts of the wider banking group before conducting its own internal searches”.
Mr Hughes said the three banks found to be in breach would be followed up this year. If the BCCC concluded that they had failed to act, they could face a referral to the Australian Securities & Investments Commission. “It’s up to them now,” he said.
